records.ety: [OE. wideren; probably the same word as wederen to weather (see Weather, v. & n.); or cf. G. verwittern to decay, to be weather-beaten, Lith. vysti to wither.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Withered (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Withering.]

defs: To fade; to lose freshness; to become sapless; to become sapless; to dry or shrivel up.
quotes: Shall he hot pull up the roots thereof, and cut off the fruit thereof, that it wither?

defs: To lose or want animal moisture; to waste; to pin<?/ away, as animal bodies.
quotes: This is man, old, wrinkled, faded, withered.
There was a man which had his hand withered.
Now warm in love, now with'ring in the grave.

defs: To lose vigor or power; to languish; to pass away.
quotes: States thrive or wither as moons wax and wane.

defs: To cause to fade, and become dry.
quotes: The sun is no sooner risen with a burning heat, but it withereth the grass, and the flower thereof falleth.

defs: To cause to shrink, wrinkle, or decay, for want of animal moisture.
quotes: Shot forth pernicious fire Among the accursed, that withered all their strength.

defs: To cause to languish, perish, or pass away; to blight; as, a reputation withered by calumny.
quotes: The passions and the cares that wither life.
